figures.caption: Fig. 8. Success rates of different methods on physical experiments. We compared our proposed method against three baseline approaches (Im2Flow2Act, FLIP, and π0) across five manipulation tasks and report the average success rate. Each task was evaluated over 20 trials. Bold numbers indicate the highest success rate for each task.

Fig. 2. Overview of the LILAC framework. The inputs to the flow generation module are a single RGB image ximg and a natural language instruction xinst. In this module, first, visual prompts and bounding boxes are generated by the MLLM. Subsequently, 2D flow is autoregressively generated by the Prompt-Conditioned Multi-Modal Adapter and the Semantic Reconstruction Decoder. After generating the 2D flow, based on the flow, ximg, xinst, and xdepth, a 6-DoF manipulator trajectory is generated by the Action De-Tokenizer.

Fig. 10. Qualitative results of LILAC executing tasks on the real HSR platform. In each example, the following instructions are given: (a) “Could you take the coke.” (b) “Place the cup near the orange.” (c) “Put the brick close to the bottle.” In this figure, (a) and (b) represent successful execution examples, and (c) shows a failure case. The 2D Flow column shows the 2D flow generated by the flow generation module.
Fig. 4. Qualitative results on the Robot Flow benchmark. The given xinst was (a) “Take the yellow block to put it on top of the tower.” and (b) “Close top drawer.”, respectively.

Fig. 5. Qualitative results with and without visual prompts. ‘Visual prompt’, ‘w/o Visual prompt’, and ‘w/ Visual prompt’ denote the visual prompt generated by the MLLM, the flow generated without a visual prompt, and the flow generated with a visual prompt, respectively. In each example, the given instruction sentence is as follows: (a) “Move 7up can near brown chip bag.” (b) “Pick black chip bag.”
